1: אֱלֹהִים לָנוּ מַחֲסֶה וָעֹז (ʾĕlōhîm lānû maḥăseh wāʿōz, "God is our refuge and strength") — maḥăseh: refuge, shelter (from ḥāsāh: to take refuge — the recurring trust-verb of the Psalter). ʿŌz: strength, might (as in Psalms 28:7-8; Psalms 29:1). The pairing: both shelter and strength — protection from outside threat and capacity within. עֶזְרָה בְצָרוֹת נִמְצָא מְאֹד (ʿezrāh bĕṣārôt nimṣāʾ mĕʾōd, "a help that is always present in times of trouble") — nimṣāʾ (niphal: to be found, to be available): help that is actively findable (mĕʾōd: very much, exceedingly) in the tight-place (ṣārôt: troubles, as in ṣar).
2: עַל-כֵּן לֹא-נִירָא בְּהָמִיר אָרֶץ (ʿal-kên lōʾ-nîrāʾ bĕhāmîr ʾāreṣ, "Therefore we will not fear, even if the earth gives way") — hāmar: to be changed, to be exchanged, to give way (the earth itself undergoing total transformation). וּבְמוֹט הָרִים בְּלֵב יַמִּים (ûbĕmôṭ hārîm bĕlêb yammîm, "even if the mountains topple into the heart of the sea") — māṭ: to totter, to slip (Psalms 30:6; Psalms 32:6 — the verb of being shaken). The mountains falling into the sea is the image of ultimate cosmic catastrophe — the end of the stable world order. The confession: even then, no fear.
3: יֶהֱמוּ יֶחְמְרוּ מֵימָיו (yehĕmû yeḥmĕrû mêmāyw, "Though the waters roar and churn") — hāmāh: to roar, to be turbulent (the inner noise of Psalms 42:5, now applied to the chaotic waters); ḥāmar: to foam, to ferment, to churn. יִרְעֲשׁוּ הָרִים בְּגַאֲוָתוֹ סֶלָה (yirʿăshû hārîm bĕgaʾăwātô selāh, "though the mountains quake at the surging sea") — rāʿash: to shake, to tremble (the earthquake-trembling of Psalms 18:7); gaʾăwāh: the pride/swelling surge of the sea. Selāh — pause in the face of cosmic chaos.
4: נָהָר פְּלָגָיו יְשַׂמְּחוּ עִיר-אֱלֹהִים (nāhār pĕlāgāyw yĕśammĕḥû ʿîr-ʾĕlōhîm, "There is a river whose streams bring joy to the city of God") — nāhār: a river; pĕlāgîm: its streams, channels (as in Psalms 1:3 — palgê māyim). The rivers of chaos (v3) contrast with the life-giving river of God's city. Jerusalem had no natural river — the image is the heavenly river of Eden (Genesis 2:10) or the eschatological river of Ezekiel 47:1-12 and Revelation 22:1-2. קְדֹשׁ מִשְׁכְּנֵי עֶלְיוֹן (qĕdōsh mishkĕnê ʿelyôn, "the sacred dwelling of the Most High") — the divine dwelling (the Tabernacle / temple) as the holy place of ʿElyôn (the Most High — the highest divine title).
5: אֱלֹהִים בְּקִרְבָּהּ בַּל-תִּמּוֹט (ʾĕlōhîm bĕqirbāh bal-timmôṭ, "God is within her; she will not be shaken") — qereb: midst, interior — God in the innermost part of the city. Māṭ (niphal: to be shaken, to totter): the word for instability used repeatedly in this psalm cluster — the city will not be made to totter. יַעְזְרֶהָ אֱלֹהִים לִפְנוֹת בֹּקֶר (yaʿzĕrehā ʾĕlōhîm lipnôt bōqer, "God will help her when morning breaks") — lipnôt bōqer: "at the turning of the morning" — the decisive moment when the morning breaks and the night-siege ends. Morning as the time of divine intervention (Psalms 30:5; Psalms 46:5 — the rescue comes with dawn).
6: הָמוּ גוֹיִם מָטוּ מַמְלָכוֹת (hāmû gôyim māṭû mamlākôt, "Nations raged and kingdoms shook") — hāmāh (the same roaring as the waters in v3): the nations' tumult mirrors the cosmic chaos. Māṭ (to totter/shake) applied to kingdoms — the unstable nations are the political equivalent of the unstable mountains. נָתַן בְּקוֹלוֹ תָּמוּג אָרֶץ (nātan bĕqôlô tāmûg ʾāreṣ, "he lifted his voice, and the earth dissolved") — the divine voice (as in Psalms 29:3-9 — the qôl YHWH) dissolving the earth. Māwag: to melt, to dissolve — the paradox: the earth that cannot be shaken (v2-5) because God is within it can be dissolved by his voice when he acts against the hostile nations.
7: יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת עִמָּנוּ (YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt ʿimmānû, "The LORD of hosts is with us") — the refrain (also v11). YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt: the LORD of Armies/Hosts — the covenant name combined with the military title. ʿImmānû: "with us" — the same two Hebrew components as ʿImmānûʾêl (Immanuel: God-with-us, Isaiah 7:14 / Matthew 1:23). The Immanuel promise is embedded in this refrain: the divine warrior whose name is Armies is with us. מִשְׂגָּב-לָנוּ אֱלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב סֶלָה (miśgāb-lānû ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb selāh, "the God of Jacob is our fortress") — miśgāb: high fortress, elevated stronghold (as in Psalms 18:2; Psalms 59:9). The God of Jacob — the patriarchal covenant-name — is the elevated stronghold of the community.
8: לְכוּ-חֲזוּ מִפְעֲלוֹת יְהוָה (lĕkû-ḥăzû mipʿălôt YHWH, "Come and see what the LORD has done") — ḥāzāh: to gaze, to behold (the prophetic vision-word — used for the seeing of visions, Isaiah 1:1). Mipʿălôt YHWH: the works/deeds of the LORD (the same root pāʿal as in Psalms 28:4-5). אֲשֶׁר-שָׂם שַׁמּוֹת בָּאָרֶץ (ʾăsher-śām shammôt bāʾāreṣ, "what desolations he has brought upon the earth") — shammāh: desolation, devastation — the destruction the LORD has wrought among the nations who attacked his city.
9: מַשְׁבִּית מִלְחָמוֹת עַד-קְצֵה הָאָרֶץ (mashbît milḥāmôt ʿad-qĕṣeh hāʾāreṣ, "He puts an end to wars across the whole earth") — shābat (hiphil: to cause to cease, to make stop — the same root as Sabbath/shabbāt): God making wars cease as he makes labor cease on the seventh day. The eschatological Sabbath of warfare. קֶשֶׁת יְשַׁבֵּר (qeshet yĕshabbêr, "he breaks bows") — shābar: to break, to shatter (as he shattered the cedars in Psalms 29:5 — the same divine shattering of military power). The war-implements (bows, spears, war-chariots) destroyed.
10: הַרְפּוּ וּדְעוּ כִּי-אָנֹכִי אֱלֹהִים (harpû ûdĕʿû kî-ʾānōkî ʾĕlōhîm, "Be still, and know that I am God") — rāpāh (hiphil imperative: to let drop, to relax, to release the grip — not merely "be quiet" but actively letting go): the command to release the striving, to unclench. The English "be still" captures the resulting posture but misses the active release: "Let go and know." Dĕʿû: to know — not merely intellectual assent but the experiential, covenantal knowing. אָרוּם בַּגּוֹיִם אָרוּם בָּאָרֶץ (ārûm baggôyim ārûm bāʾāreṣ, "I will be exalted among the nations; I will be exalted throughout the earth") — rûm: to be high, to be exalted. The double exaltation — among the hostile nations and throughout the entire earth — is the eschatological goal of all the divine acts of vv8-9.
11: The refrain for the second time: יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת עִמָּנוּ מִשְׂגָּב-לָנוּ אֱלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב — "The LORD of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress." The repetition of the refrain encloses the declaration of the LORD's deeds (vv8-10) in the same covenantal affirmation: before and after the description of what God does (shattering warfare, commanding rest), the community confesses who God is: the LORD of armies, with us, the God of Jacob, our high fortress. The Immanuel-name (ʿimmānû) brackets the central theology of the psalm.
